description: The figure presents the binary Fe-Al phase diagram illustrating the phase equilibria as a function of temperature (200–1600 °C) and aluminum content (0–100 wt% or 0–100 atom%). The diagram delineates multiple phase fields including the liquid phase (L), body-centered cubic α-Fe (ferrite), face-centered cubic γ-Fe (austenite), and several ordered intermetallic compounds: B2-structured FeAl, D03-structured Fe3Al, Fe2Al5, FeAl2, and FeAl3. Key invariant reactions are marked with specific temperatures, such as the peritectic at 1530 °C for α-Fe formation, the eutectoid at 770 °C, and melting points at 1310 °C, 1215 °C, 1164 °C, 1171 °C, and 1157 °C for various intermetallic phases. The diagram also indicates the magnetic transformation line at 770 °C for the α-phase. As stated in the caption and supporting text, aluminum forms a disordered solid solution in iron up to approximately 11 wt.%, beyond which brittle intermetallic phases (Fe3Al and FeAl) appear, critically influencing the design of lightweight steels by defining the solubility limit for Al addition without compromising workability.

This figure presents a density-composition plot comparing the density reduction effectiveness of aluminum in two ferrous alloy systems: binary Fe-Al and quaternary Fe-Mn-Al-C. The x-axis indicates aluminum content ranging from 0 to 12 wt.%, while the y-axis shows density from 6.5 to 8.0 g·cm⁻³. Blue circles represent Fe-Al alloys, exhibiting a linear decreasing trend from approximately 7.85 g·cm⁻³ at 0% Al to about 6.8 g·cm⁻³ at 10.5% Al. Red triangles denote Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys containing 14 wt.% Mn, showing marginally lower densities than Fe-Al counterparts at comparable aluminum levels, decreasing from roughly 7.1 to 6.7 g·cm⁻³ within the measured Al range (7-11 wt.%). The caption notes that approximately 8 wt.% Al causes a density reduction of about 10%, and manganese contributes a slight additional density decrease beyond the primary aluminum effect. This data demonstrates the substantial effectiveness of aluminum in reducing steel density for automotive lightweighting applications while establishing the secondary role of manganese in the Fe-Mn-Al-C system.

This figure presents a horizontal bar chart comparing the mean grain sizes of three Fe-Al lightweight ferritic steels against a conventional Al-free IF (interstitial-free) steel. The x-axis denotes mean grain size in micrometers ranging from 0 to 100 μm. The four materials displayed are: 9.7Al-0.04C-0.09Ti (with 0.04C underlined indicating high carbon content), 8.1Al-0.0015C-0.14Ti, 6.8Al-0.0035C-0.10Ti, and the reference IF steel. The chart reveals that low-density steels with ultralow carbon (0.0015–0.0035 wt.%) and sufficient Ti stabilizer (6.8 and 8.1 wt.% Al steels) exhibit significantly coarser grain sizes of approximately 70–90 μm, substantially larger than the traditional IF steel (~20 μm). In contrast, the 9.7 wt.% Al steel containing higher carbon (0.04 wt.%) and insufficient stabilizing element possesses a fine grain size (~18 μm) comparable to IF steel. This comparison demonstrates that carbon content critically governs grain structure in high-Al ferritic steels, where solute carbon refines grains via solute drag effects, while ultralow carbon levels permit abnormal grain growth.

This bar chart illustrates the bake hardening (BH) response of five Fe-Al ferritic lightweight steels subjected to a simulated paint baking treatment (2% pre-deformation followed by aging at 170°C for 20 min). The x-axis identifies the alloys by number (1–5), with corresponding compositions listed as: 1=7Al-0.002C-0.01Ti, 2=7Al-0.004C-0.02Nb, 3=8Al-0.003C-0.01Ti-0.01Nb, 4=7.5Al-0.004C-0.02Nb, and 5=6.5Al-0.005C-0.01Ti-0.01Nb (all in wt.%). The y-axis reports yield strength (YS) in MPa. White bars represent pre-BH YS (ranging ~340–420 MPa), while pink bars show post-BH YS (~420–500 MPa). A blue line with circular markers denotes the YS increment (ΔYS), which remains consistently around 70–80 MPa across all compositions. The figure demonstrates that proper alloy design utilizing microalloying elements (Ti, Nb) and controlled Al (6.5–8 wt.%) and C contents enables these lightweight steels to achieve significant strength enhancement via bake hardening, attributed to the locking of dislocations by interstitial carbon atoms during the aging treatment.

This figure presents a thermodynamic phase fraction diagram calculated using JMatPro 6.1 software, showing equilibrium phase amounts (in wt.%) as a function of temperature (0–1500°C) for a lean duplex lightweight steel with nominal composition Fe-6.57Al-3.3Mn-0.18C (wt.%). The plot displays four distinct phase evolution curves: Ferrite (red line) dominates at low temperatures (>95 wt.%), decreases to approximately 88 wt.% around 700–750°C due to austenite formation, then increases back toward 100 wt.% at elevated temperatures until melting initiates near 1450°C; Austenite (blue line) appears in the intercritical range (starting ~700°C) with a maximum content of approximately 10–12 wt.%, gradually dissolving above 900°C and disappearing by 1250°C; Carbides (magenta line) remain stable at low temperatures up to ~600°C at ~2 wt.% before dissolving completely; and Liquid phase (black line) emerges above ~1450°C, reaching 100 wt.% at 1500°C. This calculation reveals a unique inverse phase transformation behavior in the intercritical region where ferrite content increases with temperature while austenite decreases, contrary to conventional low-carbon steels, which is critical for designing intercritical annealing treatments in ferrite-based duplex Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels.

A schematic illustration depicting the phase transformation sequence involved in the formation of κ-carbide from ferrite in lean Mn-containing Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys during continuous cooling. The diagram traces the microstructural evolution starting from the α-ferrite matrix, followed by γ-austenite nucleation and growth. Subsequently, a ring of κ-carbide forms at the α/γ interface, which then undergoes fragmentation and globulization. The schematic shows two competing pathways at lower temperatures: either total or partial transformation of austenite to ferrite and κ-carbide through a eutectoid reaction, or the formation of a modulated structure via spinodal decomposition (γ → γ + κ-carbide). This figure elucidates the metallurgical mechanism by which triplex microstructures develop in lightweight steels, supporting the understanding of carbide precipitation kinetics at interphase boundaries.

Figure 7 presents two pseudo-binary phase diagrams showing alloying effects on κ-carbide solvus temperatures in precipitation-hardenable lightweight Fe-Mn-Al-C steels. Subfigure (a) plots temperature versus aluminum content (5.5–10.5 wt.%) for Fe-30Mn-xAl-1C alloys, delineating phase fields of single γ-austenite, γ + κ* (intergranular κ-phase), and γ + κ + κ* (intragranular plus intergranular κ-phase). The phase boundaries reveal that increasing Al content raises the solvus temperature for both precipitation modes, expanding the single-phase austenite region toward higher temperatures. Subfigure (b) plots temperature versus manganese content (23–30 wt.%) for Fe-xMn-7Al-1C alloys, showing the same three-phase sequence but with opposite trend: increasing Mn slightly depresses the κ-carbide solvus temperatures. These complementary diagrams demonstrate that Al stabilizes κ-carbide precipitation at higher temperatures whereas Mn mildly destabilizes it, providing critical thermodynamic guidance for optimizing aging treatments in the 500–750°C range to achieve precipitation strengthening in automotive lightweight steels.

Figure 8 presents transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images illustrating the microstructure development in Fe-(26-28)Mn-(11-12)Al-(0.9-1.15)C (wt.%) triplex steels after room-temperature tensile deformation, demonstrating the Shear Induced Plasticity (SIP) effect. Image (a) is a bright-field TEM micrograph showing distinct shear bands aligned on {111} crystallographic planes within the austenite matrix. Image (b) is a dark-field TEM image captured using the superlattice reflection (210) of the [001] fcc zone axis, revealing a regular, periodic arrangement of nanosized j-carbides (κ-carbides) with dimensions of 20–30 nm that maintain coherency with the austenitic matrix. The images collectively demonstrate that the uniform distribution of coherent nanoscale κ-carbides gives rise to homogeneously arranged shear bands, which is attributed to the highly stable austenite possessing high positive Gibbs energy (1755 J mol⁻¹) for austenite-to-martensite transformation and relatively high stacking fault energy (110 mJ m⁻²). This microstructural configuration validates the SIP deformation mechanism in these lightweight triplex steels.

Figure 9 presents a strength-elongation 'banana diagram' comparing the mechanical property ranges of various low-density steel categories, with ultimate tensile strength (MPa) on the x-axis and total elongation (%) on the y-axis. The plot displays five distinct material groups: Fe-Al ferritic steels (red ellipse, ~500-650 MPa, ~15-30% elongation), Fe-Mn-Al-C duplex steels (α+γ, blue ellipse, ~600-800 MPa, ~35-45% elongation), Fe-Mn-Al-C precipitation-hardening (PH) steels (α+κ, blue ellipse, ~800-900 MPa, ~10-20% elongation), Fe-Mn-Al-C triplex steels (γ+α+κ, blue ellipse, ~850-1000 MPa, ~40-70% elongation), and Fe-Al intermetallics (purple ellipse, ~950-1000 MPa, ~5-15% elongation). Density reduction percentages are indicated as 7.5-12.7% for ferritic steels, 10-20% for Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, and >25% for intermetallics. The figure demonstrates that austenite-containing duplex and triplex steels achieve superior strength-ductility balances compared to ferritic steels while offering significant density reductions, supporting their potential for automotive lightweighting applications.

The figure presents engineering stress-strain curves of four ferritic low-density steels comparing their tensile deformation behaviors. The curves represent: (i) 9.7Al-0.04C-0.09Ti (magenta), exhibiting pronounced discontinuous yielding with serrated flow at approximately 550-620 MPa; (ii) 8.1Al-0.0015C-0.14Ti (blue) with intermediate strength and continuous yielding; (iii) 6.8Al-0.0035C-0.10Ti (red) showing lower yield strength around 350 MPa; and (iv) conventional interstitial-free (IF) steel (black) displaying the lowest strength (~150 MPa yield) but superior ductility exceeding 45% strain. The 9.7Al-0.04C-0.09Ti alloy demonstrates distinct yield point elongation characteristic of free carbon in ferritic matrices, contrasting with continuous yielding in high-Al and IF alloys. As Al and C contents decrease, both yield strength and ultimate tensile strength systematically decrease from ~620 MPa to ~300 MPa. The figure supports the metallurgical principle that free interstitial carbon causes discontinuous yielding, which produces surface stretcher marks during sheet forming, necessitating carbon stabilization through Ti addition for automotive formability.

The figure presents a mechanical property plot showing the true stress-true strain curve and the corresponding strain hardening rate as a function of true strain for an MBIP (Micro-band Induced Plasticity) steel with nominal composition Fe-28Mn-9Al-0.8C. The red curve represents the true stress-true strain relationship, indicating continuous flow stress increase from approximately 450 MPa to over 1600 MPa up to a true strain of 0.7. The blue curve depicts the strain hardening rate evolution, which starts at an exceptionally high value near 2400 MPa, initially drops to a minimum around 1550 MPa at 0.05 strain, then rises to a broad peak of approximately 1900 MPa between true strains of 0.4-0.5, followed by a gradual decline. This distinct high work-hardening rate behavior, contrasting with ferritic steels, characterizes austenite-containing low-density steels and reflects the operation of ductility-enhancing deformation mechanisms such as mechanical twinning or micro-band formation during plastic deformation.

The figure presents a line graph illustrating the degradation of Young's modulus in low-density steels as a function of aluminum content (0–10 wt.%). Two distinct datasets are compared: blue circular markers connected by a line represent data from Rana et al. [6, 20], while red triangular markers denote results from Frommeyer et al. [18]. The x-axis indicates Al content in weight percent, and the y-axis shows Young's modulus in GPa ranging from 140 to 220. The blue curve demonstrates a continuous nearly linear decrease from approximately 205 GPa at 0% Al to about 153 GPa at 10% Al. The red curve covers a narrower composition range (approximately 7–9 wt.% Al), showing values decreasing from roughly 191 GPa to 170 GPa. This quantitative relationship reveals that Al addition, while beneficial for density reduction, significantly compromises the elastic stiffness of ferrous alloys. The caption and accompanying text highlight this as a critical drawback for automotive body-in-white applications, where Young's modulus directly governs structural stiffness, potentially offsetting the weight reduction benefits despite the comparable mechanical properties of high-Al steels.

This flow chart illustrates the typical laboratory-scale thermomechanical processing route for producing Fe-Al-(C) and Fe-Al-Mn-C ferritic and multiphase sheet steels. The process begins with ingot casting, followed by reheating at 1250°C and rough rolling. After a second reheating at 1250°C, hot rolling is performed to a final thickness of 3.5 mm with a finish rolling temperature (FRT) of 900°C, followed by run-out table cooling and coiling. The coiled strip then undergoes pickling at 85°C using HCl, followed by cold rolling or warm rolling at 250°C to a final thickness of 1 mm. Subsequently, continuous annealing is conducted at 850-900°C, with a final aging step at 400-700°C specifically for Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys. The diagram indicates that while these processing steps correspond to industrial routes used for conventional automotive steels, the thermomechanical parameters (temperature, cooling rate, rolling reduction) must be carefully selected based on alloy chemistry—particularly the coiling temperature, which ranges from high temperatures (~650°C) for ultralow C Fe-Al-(C) alloys to room temperature for high-alloy Fe-Mn-Al-C steels—to suppress brittle κ-carbide precipitation and ensure adequate formability.

Figure 14 presents finite element simulation results of an engineering case study comparing shell thickness distributions in the Body-in-White (BIW) of a Volkswagen Golf V when replacing conventional HSLA steels with a ferrite-based duplex low-density steel (Fe-6.57Al-3.3Mn-0.18C). Subfigure (a) displays the shell thickness distribution using HSLA steels, ranging from 0.750 mm (white) to 3.000 mm (magenta) as indicated by the color legend. Subfigure (b) shows the corresponding thickness distribution for the low-density steel alternative, with a shifted range from 0.600 mm to 4.000 mm. The visual comparison reveals an overall shift toward lower thickness values for the low-density steel design, necessitated by its lower Young's modulus (181 GPa) compared to conventional steels. The simulation maintained constant torsional stiffness as the design constraint. Despite the thickness adjustments required to compensate for reduced stiffness, the lower density (7.27 g/cm³) of the duplex steel enables a predicted 14.5% weight reduction in the BIW, demonstrating the lightweight potential of low-density steels in automotive applications without compromising structural performance.
